main_damage_causes: Nervous, genuine social anxiety replaced with deadpan comedy, misrepresenting Belby's core trait
Physical distress scene (choking) entirely absent, removing the most memorable and characterizing book moment
Belby used as background comic relief rather than a minor but distinct character with real discomfort

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 2 - Belby's response about his uncle
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Belby is thin, nervous, and visibly distressed when asked about his uncle - he chokes on food, eyes streaming, and gives a strained, uncomfortable 'Not... not much of him, no' - conveying genuine family estrangement and social anxiety
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Belby delivers a deadpan, almost comedic line about his dad thinking potions are rubbish and trails off into a joke about 'a stiff one at the end of the day' - reframing his discomfort as dry humor rather than genuine nervous distress
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book's Belby is genuinely awkward and pained about his family situation; the film turns this into a throwaway gag, losing the authentic social anxiety and the pathos of a kid clearly uncomfortable with his family estrangement

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 2 - Belby never looks up from his plate
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Belby's nervousness is expressed through physical distress (choking) and strained speech, not silent withdrawal
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Stage direction notes Belby 'never once looks up from his plate' throughout the scene, reducing him to a background prop who delivers one joke line
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book gives Belby small but distinct reactive moments; the film collapses him into near-invisibility except for a single comedic beat, flattening what little characterization he has

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 2 - Slughorn's dessert joke at Belby's expense
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: No such joke exists; Slughorn's interest in Belby is genuine (if transactional) - he's probing for a connection to Damocles Belby and is somewhat sympathetic when the connection proves weak
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Slughorn makes a joke implying Belby is a greedy eater ('if Belby leaves you any'), using him as comic relief
damaging_scenes.explanation: Reduces Belby further to a sight gag, undermining even the minor dignity the book affords him as a nervous but earnest student
